EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few inches of water was reported in a few houses and in some units of an apartment complex.
Flash flooding of low lying areas and roadways reported in the southern portion of Baton Rouge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 6 inches caused the flooding of three school and a few homes in the Carrollton and Broadmoor areas. Numerous roadways and a few vehicles were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 6 inches caused the flooding of three homes and a few businesses in Metairie and one home in Bridge City. Numerous roadways and a few vehicles were flooded.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms and heavy rain occurred in advance of a cold front that moved across southeast Louisiana.

Widespread heavy rain fell across across extreme southeast Louisiana in advance of a deep upper level low pressure system and a strong cold front. Rainfall amounts ranging from three to eight inches were common in an area extending from Terrebonne and Lafourche Parishes northeast across the new orleans metro area to St. Tammany Parish. Street flooding was widespread and a number of vehicles and a few homes and businesses were flooded. The worst flooding occurred in Orleans Parish and northern sections of Jefferson Parish. Waist deep water was reported on some streets in New Orleans.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall flooded 5 homes.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms that produced heavy rain developed in a moist and unstable atmosphere as an upper level disturbance moved across the Gulf Coast region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4 to 5 inches from a thunderstorm resulted in significant street flooding and made some roadways impassable. Numerous vehicles received flood damage. A few businesses took on water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4 to 6 inches from a thunderstorm resulted in widespread street flooding and the closing of some roadways. Numerous vehicles were flooded. At least 34 residences reported some damage from the flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding was observed in Destrehan, Montz and Norco. A protion of Ormond Boulevard was closed due to high water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed over southeast Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 6 to 8 inches in a little over two hours resulted in widespread street flooding in the Ama, Hahnville, Destrehan, St. Rose and New Sarpy areas. A dozen homes received minor flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed over southeast Louisiana in a moist and unstable atmosphere as a strong upper level storm system affected the lower Mississippi Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall flooded numerous roadways and some homes and vehicles on the Westbank of Jefferson Parish.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of widespread severe weather and heavy rainfall occurred from March 26th through the 28th as a series of strong upper air disturbances impacted the central gulf region as a meandering and nearly stationary frontal boundary drifted back and forth across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets were flooded due to heavy rain and numerous vehicles were under water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of vehicles were stranded in a flooded underpass at Downman Road and Haynes Boulevard. Some street flooding also occurred near Lakefront Airport.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance situated over Texas resulted in numerous showers and a few thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. The showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced widespread and significant street flooding and caused the flooding of some homes in the Fountainbleau neighborhood. One person drowned in the Algiers area of New Orleans when he drove into a flooded canal. A passenger in the auto escaped injury but the driver was unable to exit the auto.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred across sections of the New Orleans metro area as an upper air disturbance acted on deep moisture in place across the Gulf Coast. The rain fell on nearly saturated ground resulting in some flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in widespread and significant street flooding throughout northern Jefferson Parish and caused the flooding of some homes on the East Bank.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred across sections of the New Orleans metro area as an upper air disturbance acted on deep moisture in place across the Gulf Coast. The rain fell on nearly saturated ground resulting in some flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in widespread and significant street flooding and caused the flooding of a few homes around Destrehan.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred across sections of the New Orleans metro area as an upper air disturbance acted on deep moisture in place across the Gulf Coast. The rain fell on nearly saturated ground resulting in some flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in widespread street flooding. Sections of Veterans Boulevard were covered by 12 inches of water while areas of Airline Highway were covered by 18 inches of water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain fell on saturated ground across areas of southeast Louisiana, primarily to the south and southwest and Lake Pontchartrain. Isolated severe weather also occurred with some of the thunderstorms. The heavy rain and severe weather resulted from a series of upper level disturbances moving across the region in combination with a stationary frontal boundary draped across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in widespread street flooding with several roads closed due to high water in the Destrehan, Boutte, and St. Rose areas. In Boutte, 15 homes were flooded and about 100 others were threatened by flood waters.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain fell on saturated ground across areas of southeast Louisiana, primarily to the south and southwest and Lake Pontchartrain. Isolated severe weather also occurred with some of the thunderstorms. The heavy rain and severe weather resulted from a series of upper level disturbances moving across the region in combination with a stationary frontal boundary draped across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding with streets impassible near the intersection of Tulane Avenue and Jefferson Davis Parkway.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed on a lake breeze in the New Orleans Metro area. The storms produced small hail and flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding reported on Transcontinental...David Drive...West Metairie and Clearview Roads.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed on a lake breeze in the New Orleans Metro area. The storms produced small hail and flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2.5 inches of rain between 8 am and 10 am at City Hall. Numerous roads were reported to be impassible. Cars were floating in several intersections.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced reports of wind damage and flash flooding in the New Orleans Metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded Wooddale Avenue with flood water entering a food market.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain resulting in minor flooding in the Baton Rouge area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water intruded into the first floor of an apartment comlex in southeast Metairie near Ochsner Medical Center. There were also several flooded streets in Bridge City.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorms associated with the remnant circulation of Tropical Depression 5 produced produced heavy rain across Metro New Orleans and some flooding in the Metairie area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of street flooding were received in Baton Rouge.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms associated with Tropical Depression Five produced flooding in Pointe Coupee and East Baton Rouge Parishes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of rain produced flooding of five homes in Des Allemands, Bayou Gauche, Boutte and Paradis.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced isolated flooding south and west of New Orleans.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two businesses were flooded near Jefferson Highway and Metairie Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on many roads. A nursing home had water coming under the doorway in Greenwell Springs.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in advance of a strong cold front produced numerous reports of flooding and severe weather on the afternoon of the 8th through the morning of the 9th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: WAFB-TV in Baton Rouge reported extensive street flooding in the Old Scenic Highway area. Several vehicles were submerged. The entrance to Sweet Briar Mobile Home Park was impassible.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in advance of a strong cold front produced numerous reports of flooding and severe weather on the afternoon of the 8th through the morning of the 9th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple streets reported flooded with water in 2 houses.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in advance of a strong cold front produced numerous reports of flooding and severe weather on the afternoon of the 8th through the morning of the 9th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was overflowing the banks of the canal between West Napoleon and Severn.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in advance of a strong cold front produced numerous reports of flooding and severe weather on the afternoon of the 8th through the morning of the 9th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were flooded around Harvey along Manhattan and Lapalco Boulevards. Two to three feet of water was reported on Gretna Blvd. Water was approaching, but not moving into, homes in this area.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level disturbances moving over a very warm and unstable airmass produced numerous reports of severe weather, as well as isolated flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jefferson Parish Emergency Management reported water in 25 apartments in the 1600 block of Carol Sue in Terrytown.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level disturbances moving over a very warm and unstable airmass produced numerous reports of severe weather, as well as isolated flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure combined with an extremely moist airmass to produce thunderstorms that remained nearly stationary over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi during the early morning hours of July 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass aided the development of scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon hours each day. The slow movement of the thunderstorms produced several reports of flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police reported street flooding of Loyola Drive and West Esplanade. Hooper Drive at Loyola Drive was also reported flooded.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass aided the development of scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon hours each day. The slow movement of the thunderstorms produced several reports of flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding beginning to be reported throughout St. Charles Parish.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front provided a focus for several waves of thunderstorms and heavy rain over a 3 day period. Several reports of tornadoes and flash flooding were received during the period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of standing water was reported in sections of Gentilly. Cars were stalled out in the flood water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low centered over Oklahoma combined with a very unstable airmass which allowed numerous severe thunderstorms to develop over southeast Louisiana during the overnight hours on the 3rd into the 4th and again the following night.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports water entering at least one building near Mark Lane and Citrus Drive in River Ridge.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low centered over Oklahoma combined with a very unstable airmass which allowed numerous severe thunderstorms to develop over southeast Louisiana during the overnight hours on the 3rd into the 4th and again the following night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, unstable airmass combined with an upper level disturbance to produce strong thunderstorms during the morning and afternoon hours. These thunderstorms were slow moving and produced very heavy rainfall in isolated areas.

The Loyola Avenue onramp to the Pontchartrain Expressway was underwater. Areas of around South Jefferson Davis Parkway also had significant and deep street flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary upper level trough and a tropical airmass produced very slow moving thunderstorms with torrential rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were impassible due to flash flooding in River Ridge, Harahan and Metairie.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were reported flooded throughout the metropolitan area. Rain rates of 2 inches per hour were reported during the peak rainfall between 130 pm and 230 pm CDT.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front triggered the development of severe thunderstorms during the late morning and afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with Hurricane Isaac produced deep street flooding in the New Orleans area.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isaac entered the Gulf of Mexico as a tropical storm on August 26, moving northwest after crossing Haiti, Cuba and the Florida Straits. Isaac strengthened into a hurricane on the morning of the 28th when it was 75 miles south-southeast of the mouth of the Mississippi River.  Isaac made landfall in Plaquemines Parish as a Category 1 Hurricane near Southwest Pass of the Mississippi River on the evening of the 28th. A second landfall occurred near Port Fourchon the following morning. The storm weakened to a tropical storm on the afternoon of the 29th about 50 miles west southwest of New Orleans, and weakened further to a tropical depression on the afternoon of the 30th near Monroe, Louisiana. || The highest wind gust recorded on land in Louisiana was 75 knots, or 86 mph, measured by a portable weather station (Texas Tech University) near Buras on the evening at August 28.  The maximum sustained wind in Louisiana was 65 knots, or 75 mph, at the same portable weather station near Buras on the evening of August 28.  There were several marine observations near the coast that had slightly higher wind readings, but their observation heights were generally 80 ft or higher. ||Due to Isaac���s  very large size, and slow forward speed, tropical storm force winds lasted in excess of 48 hours in many areas of coastal southeast Louisiana. Occasional hurricane gusts of 70 to 85 mph were recorded across southeast Louisiana during the night of the Aug 28th and early on the 29th, especially south of Lake Pontchartrain. Interior areas of southeast Louisiana such as around Baton Rouge and northward experienced tropical storm force winds. Widespread power outages occurred across the area. Local utility companies reported over 700,000 customers were without power at the peak of the storm in southeast Louisiana.  Generally, most of the wind damage was limited to downed trees and power lines, and roof damage caused by wind and falling trees and tree limbs.||The minimum sea level pressure measured from a land station was 965 mb on the morning of Aug 29 at a portable weather station ( Texas Tech Univ) located near Larose.   A pressure of 974.3 mb was measured at a NOAA gage at Bayou Gauche on the morning of August 29th.  Close to the landfall location, a reading 969.8 mb was measured at the NOAA NOS station at Pilots Station near Southwest Pass  on the evening of the  29th shortly after the first landfall. The height of this measurement was 24 meter or 79 feet. ||A storm tide of 8 to 13 feet  occurred in eastern Plaquemines Parish and St. Bernard Parish.  A representative maximum storm tide of 13.21 ft NAVD88 was measured at a USGS tide gage near Pointe a la Hache. A local levee was overtopped or breached in the Braithwaite area of Plaquemines Parish early on August 29. A number of people had to be rescued from the flooded area, but a 60 year old woman and 52 year old man drowned in the storm surge. ||Significant impact also occurred around Lakes Pontchartrain and Maurepas with a storm tide of 5 to 9  feet. 5 to 10 thousand homes were flooded in low lying areas of that border these lakes of the following parishes: St. Tammany, Tangipahoa, Livingston, Ascension, St James and St John the Baptist.   Laplace in St. John the Baptist was especially hard hit with over 5,000 homes flooded by storm surge.  An additional storm surge fatality occurred in St. Tammany Parish on the morning of the 30th when a 75 year old man drove his car into a storm surge filled ditch. ||Storm surge flooding also affected areas south and southwest of New Orleans with a storm tide of 4 to 7 feet. Roadways and low lying property were flooded. Local levees around Lafitte and Myrtle Grove were overtopped and/or breached resulting flooding of numerous houses and property in this area.||Many areas of southeast Louisiana received 8 to 12 inches of rain with a few locations having 15 inches of rain or more.  Maximum storm total rainfall was 20.66 inches at the New Orleans Carrollton gauge on the Mississippi River.  Rainfall run-off produced moderate to major flooding on the Tangipahoa, Tchefuncte, Tickfaw, Amite, Pearl, Bogue Chitto and Bogue Falaya Rivers.  Storm surge and high tides restricted outflow of the rivers near the coast and lakes exacerbating flooding in those areas.   ||Overall  impacts of Isaac resulted in at least $600 million in damages in southeast Louisiana, 3 direct fatalities, and 2 indirect fatalities.  Storm surge flooding accounted for the bulk of damage, estimated around $500 million and the three direct storm surge fatalities in Louisiana. Winds accounted for a much lesser amount of slightly more than a $100 million. As stated earlier, these are early damage estimates and will likely be adjusted upward due to later damage assessments.||During the recovery process there were two indirect fatalities. On Sept 1, A 67 year man was electrocuted when attempting to restore power to his house in Abita Springs, St Tammany Parish. On Sept 3, a 90 year old man died of heat related impacts in his house in Marrero, Jefferson Parish, where power had not been restored.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper low over southern Louisiana fueled the development of numerous thunderstorms across the area. Several reports of flooding were received in the New Orleans area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee witnessed cars stalling out due to flash flooding in downtown New Orleans.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist and unstable air surged northward over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi ahead of a cold front moving through the Great Plains. Thunderstorms developed in this moist airmass, producing very heavy rainfall, which caused flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local news media showed live footage of multiple high water rescues across the city of New Orleans.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist and unstable air surged northward over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi ahead of a cold front moving through the Great Plains. Thunderstorms developed in this moist airmass, producing very heavy rainfall, which caused flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread street flooding was reported in Harahan. Some roads were reported impassible.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southeast Louisiana provided the focus for the development of several rounds of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Torrential rain produced several reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous flooded and impassable streets in the Luling and St. Rose areas.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southeast Louisiana provided the focus for the development of several rounds of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Torrential rain produced several reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous flooded and impassable streets were reported in East New Orleans with several cars stalled in the flood waters.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southeast Louisiana provided the focus for the development of several rounds of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Torrential rain produced several reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orleans Parish Emergency Management reported extensive street flooding in New Orleans Lakeview, Lakefront and Mid City areas with some streets impassible.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding during the afternoon of the 31st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant and deep street street flooding was reported by the public, state transportation agency and social media in various parts of Baton Rouge including the following streets: North Street, College Drive, Sherwood Forest Blvd, Goodwood Drive, and Perkins Road.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper air disturbance aided the development of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana during the late morning and early afternoon of the 9th. The thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in the Baton Rouge area which resulted in several reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Bluebonnet Road and Jefferson Highway area, at least one vehicle was flooded out with occupants rescued, and water reported in at least one structure. Flooding was also reported on the Jefferson Highway and Airline Highway area and on Sherwood Forest and Coursey Blvd.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary developed from a complex of severe thunderstorms over southern Alabama and Mississippi during the early morning hours of the 24th. This boundary served as the focus for development of severe thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi during the afternoon hours. Reports of wind damage and flash flooding were reported over southeast Louisiana during the afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were reported impassible with several cars flooded in Metairie and Elmwood.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved into a very unstable airmass over Louisiana, Mississippi and the northern Gulf of Mexico. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front during the day. At least one waterspout was reported, as well as hail and strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were reported impassible with several cars flooded in the Mid City and Lakeview areas.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved into a very unstable airmass over Louisiana, Mississippi and the northern Gulf of Mexico. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front during the day. At least one waterspout was reported, as well as hail and strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flash flooding throughout Destrehan, Boutte, Luling and Paradis. Numerous roads were closed in these areas and 15 homes flooded.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to the area. The system tapped into deep tropical moisture resulting in very intense rainfall across a large portion of the area. Two-day rainfall totals of 10 inches or more were common across areas generally to the west and northwest of Lakes Pontchartrain and Maurepas. The heaviest rainfall occurred across a swath of East Baton Rouge, Livingston, and St. Helena Parishes, where 20 to 30 inches fell over a 48-hour period, topping the 0.1% annual exceedance probability. The heavy rainfall led to widespread flash flooding and record river flooding. Many of the broken records had been set during the flooding of April 1983. In total, 12 people died in southeast Louisiana as a result of the flooding. The event was also responsible for an estimated total of ten billion dollars in damage across southern Louisiana and southern Mississippi. This estimate includes impacts of both the initial flash flooding and the resultant river flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty to thirty inches of rainfall over a 2 day period led to widespread flash flooding in East Baton Rouge Parish. As water drained into the local rivers, record levels were recorded. Additional details of the river flooding can be found in the flood report for the parish. As floodwaters rose, hundreds of high water rescues were necessary across the parish. Portions of interstates 10 and 110 were closed due to water crossing the roadway during the flash flooding. In all, approximately 25,000 homes and hundreds of additional businesses suffered various degrees of flooding, though it's difficult to determine how many were due to initial flash flooding and how many were due to subsequent river flooding. During the flash flooding, a 68 year old man drowned when he slipped into a drainage ditch trying to escape his home in Zachary, LA Friday morning.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to the area. The system tapped into deep tropical moisture resulting in very intense rainfall across a large portion of the area. Two-day rainfall totals of 10 inches or more were common across areas generally to the west and northwest of Lakes Pontchartrain and Maurepas. The heaviest rainfall occurred across a swath of East Baton Rouge, Livingston, and St. Helena Parishes, where 20 to 30 inches fell over a 48-hour period, topping the 0.1% annual exceedance probability. The heavy rainfall led to widespread flash flooding and record river flooding. Many of the broken records had been set during the flooding of April 1983. In total, 12 people died in southeast Louisiana as a result of the flooding. The event was also responsible for an estimated total of ten billion dollars in damage across southern Louisiana and southern Mississippi. This estimate includes impacts of both the initial flash flooding and the resultant river flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell across central and northern Baton Rouge. The rainfall produced high water along Scenic Highway and Harding Boulevard.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm dumped heavy rain in the Baton Rouge area producing localized flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Louisiana Department of Transportation reported US Highway 61 at Louisiana Highway 67 was closed due to flooding. Report was relayed by social media.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front provided a focus for the development of slow moving thunderstorms in the Baton Rouge area that produced 5 to 6 inches of rain locally. A few areas of flooding were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were reported flooded with water entering at least two homes in St. Rose.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary moving southward across Mississippi collided with a boundary left from the remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy over southeast Louisiana. The resulting heavy rainfall produced at least one report of flash flooding in St. Charles Parish.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home received ankle deep water in Norco. There was significant street flooding reported with some roads impassable in Norco, Destrehan and Luling. Minor street flooding was reported in St. Rose. Rain gauges measured 2 to 3 inches of rain in a very short period of time in the flooded area.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weaken frontal boundary along the Interstate 10 corridor in southeast Louisiana and south Mississippi along with local land-lake/sea breeze interaction aided in the development of thunderstorms producing heavy rain along this corridor on all 3 days. Reports of flash flooding were received from the New Orleans area on the 4th and 5th, and the Gulfport-Biloxi area on the 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms repeatedly developed over the same area of New Orleans from the mid afternoon into early evening producing 4 to 9 inches of rain during a 3 to 5 hour period. The rainfall overwhelmed the drainage system leading to widespread and deep street flooding. Many streets were impassable from the Mid City area to just north of the French Quarter and Central Business District, and road and highway underpasses were deeply flooded . Numerous automobiles were also damaged by the flooding.  Water entered at least several hundred homes and a number of businesses, with estimates likely to increase as claims are processed.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weaken frontal boundary along the Interstate 10 corridor in southeast Louisiana and south Mississippi along with local land-lake/sea breeze interaction aided in the development of thunderstorms producing heavy rain along this corridor on all 3 days. Reports of flash flooding were received from the New Orleans area on the 4th and 5th, and the Gulfport-Biloxi area on the 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were reported flooded around City Park and Lakeview toward the University of New Orleans. The report was relayed by social media.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough of low pressure combined with a very moist airmass to aid the development of nearly stationary thunderstorms, which produced one report of flash flooding in the New Orleans area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 10 residences were reported to have water in them. Numerous roads across St. Charles Parish were reported to have water over them and were impassible.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Harvey struck southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana, outer rain bands moved across southeast Louisiana, southern Mississippi and the adjacent coastal waters on several occasions between the 28th and 30th of August. Several tornadoes were reported across the area as well as instances of flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jefferson Parish Sheriff's Office reported there was flood water over Veterans Boulevard at the Causeway Boulevard Overpass. Several other streets were reported impassible in Metairie.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moving westward across the northern Gulf of Mexico produced localized very heavy rainfall over southeast Louisiana during the morning hours of the 2nd. Several reports of significant street flooding were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported cars stalled in flood waters at underpass near Paris Avenue and Gentilly Boulevard.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moving westward across the northern Gulf of Mexico produced localized very heavy rainfall over southeast Louisiana during the morning hours of the 2nd. Several reports of significant street flooding were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported cars were stalled in flood waters at the Canal Boulevard Underpass near Homedale Street.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moving westward across the northern Gulf of Mexico produced localized very heavy rainfall over southeast Louisiana during the morning hours of the 2nd. Several reports of significant street flooding were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Louisiana State Climatologist reported that Dawson Creek at Perkins Road Community Park was out of its banks due to heavy rainfall.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moving across the Lower Mississippi River Valley produced an area of rain and slow moving thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. Several thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved across East Baton Rouge Parish, producing a few reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported on the south side of Baton Rouge. Some streets with high water were barricaded near Sherwood, and along interstate service roads.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moving across the Lower Mississippi River Valley produced an area of rain and slow moving thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. Several thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved across East Baton Rouge Parish, producing a few reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a home flooded in the 12500 block of Hooper Road in Central.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moving across the Lower Mississippi River Valley produced an area of rain and slow moving thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. Several thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved across East Baton Rouge Parish, producing a few reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive street flooding was reported in the Central City, Treme and Bywater neighborhoods of New Orleans. Radar rainfall estimates indicated that 3 to 5 inches of rain fell in a swath from Metairie through City Park and Mid-City, into the Bywater and Lower 9th Ward. Some vehicles were flooded at the height of the storm. Most of the rain fell between 330 pm and 600 pm.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure over the central Gulf Coast combined with increasingly moist and unstable air to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic cameras showed vehicles submerged in a flooded underpass on Interstate 110 at North 22nd.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over the Baton Rouge area during the late afternoon hours of the 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of street flooding resulting in water rescues across the area.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Chandeleur Sound produced slow moving thunderstorms which produced at least 2 reports of flash flooding in the New Orleans metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several social media reports were received of standing water on some city streets in New Orleans.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Chandeleur Sound produced slow moving thunderstorms which produced at least 2 reports of flash flooding in the New Orleans metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media posts from local broadcast media outlets showed water entering vehicles in the New Orleans Central Business District.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Collisions between thunderstorm outflow boundaries and lake breeze boundaries produced nearly stationary thunderstorms south of Lake Pontchartrain. Several reports of flooding were received from the New Orleans Metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was reported flooded in St. Rose. Numerous other roads in the parish reported street flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Collisions between thunderstorm outflow boundaries and lake breeze boundaries produced nearly stationary thunderstorms south of Lake Pontchartrain. Several reports of flooding were received from the New Orleans Metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of significant roadway flooding was received in the Baton Rouge area due to heavy rainfall. Interstate Highway 110 on the north side of Baton Rouge was closed due to flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through the Southern Plains produced widespread heavy rainfall during the 27th and 28th. Several reports of significant flooding were received near and north of Interstate 10 during the afternoon and overnight hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, with isolated amounts over 7 inches caused widespread flooding of streets and roads on the east bank of St Charles Parish. Parish emergency management reported 58 homes were flooded.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving through the Southern Plains produced widespread heavy rainfall during the 27th and 28th. Several reports of significant flooding were received near and north of Interstate 10 during the afternoon and overnight hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches within an hour or two caused small stream and drainage ditches to come out of their banks resulting in flooding of multiple side roads in the vicinity of Bluebonnet and Perkins Road.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moving from Texas to the Lower Ohio River Valley generated a line of thunderstorms that moved through southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the afternoon hours of the 18th. A wake low developed after the passage of the front, producing several areas of strong winds and minor wind damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports were received of widespread flooding within the City of Baton Rouge. Rainfall estimates of 3 to 3.25 inches of rain had fallen as of report time. Multiple streams and creeks were reported overflowing.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A constant stream of tropical moisture was acted on by a very slowly moving cold front. Several rounds of thunderstorms developed, producing severe weather and a few reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of widespread street flooding with several secondary road closures across the Parish. Estimates that up to 2 inches of rain had fallen as of report time.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A constant stream of tropical moisture was acted on by a very slowly moving cold front. Several rounds of thunderstorms developed, producing severe weather and a few reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 300 block of Green Briar Drive was reported impassable. Several reports were received of water over the road along Patriot Street.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front combined with a strong upper air impulse and very moist air produced multiple bands of thunderstorms. Several areas received multiple rounds of thunderstorms which produced flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads and streets were reported closed in the City of New Orleans due to flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front combined with a strong upper air impulse and very moist air produced multiple bands of thunderstorms. Several areas received multiple rounds of thunderstorms which produced flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Charles Parish Emergency Manager reported water entering a home on St. Rose Avenue. Several neighboring streets were flooded with some closed and barricaded. Parish gauges reported 3 to 4 inches of rain, mostly since 4 am CDT.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front combined with a strong upper air impulse and very moist air produced multiple bands of thunderstorms. Several areas received multiple rounds of thunderstorms which produced flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in the Destrehan and Norco areas.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front combined with a strong upper air impulse and very moist air produced multiple bands of thunderstorms. Several areas received multiple rounds of thunderstorms which produced flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson Parish Emergency Manager reported localized street flooding across northern portions of the parish.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front combined with a strong upper air impulse and very moist air produced multiple bands of thunderstorms. Several areas received multiple rounds of thunderstorms which produced flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4 to 7 inches occurred across the Baton Rouge metro area producing widespread flooding of streets and some buildings.  A number of roadway underpasses were flooded with cars trapped or flooded.  One fatality, a 37 year old man, occurred at the entrance ramp at Chippewa and Interstate 110 when the victims car was trapped in rising floodwaters. Emergency management officials estimated that around 145 vehicles were flooded and 11 parish buildings had minor flooding.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front along the Interstate 10-12 corridor provided the focus for the development of severe thunderstorms during the morning hours. Several tornadoes occurred over southeast Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches created significant street flooding in several areas of Jefferson Parish.  Water entered a building near Jefferson Highway in the Harahan area.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough, which would eventually become associated with Hurricane Barry, interacted with a tropically very moist airmass to produce slow moving thunderstorms on the 9th and 10th. Several reports of flooding, as well as wind damage were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of rain fell in less than three hours across portions of the area during the morning commute period produced widespread street flooding across much of New Orleans including French Quarter, Central Business District,  and Uptown areas.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough, which would eventually become associated with Hurricane Barry, interacted with a tropically very moist airmass to produce slow moving thunderstorms on the 9th and 10th. Several reports of flooding, as well as wind damage were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets reported street flooding throughout the city of New Orleans, mostly in the Central Business District and French Quarter areas.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced localized flash flooding over the city of New Orleans during the early afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported to a home in St. Rose. Rainfall totals across St. Charles Parish ranged from 2.5 to 4 inches during the evening hours.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonable cold front moved southward into coastal Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorms produced localized very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell across part of New Orleans extending into St Bernard Parish during the mid afternoon resulting in widespread street flooding was reported with numerous streets impassible. Several cars were stalled in the flood waters. Water was reported entering several buildings in the Central Business District of New Orleans.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary thunderstorms produced flash flooding in New Orleans Central Business District during the late afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parish Emergency Manager reports rain gauge near Boutte has recorded 7.5 inches of rain the evening of the 10th. Several roads flooded with some impassible across the west bank of the parish in Paradis, Boutte, and Luling.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convergent axis of anomalously high moisture brought heavy rain and flooding across parts of Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets and atleast 120 homes flooded across the City of Destrehan.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms delivered damaging winds and flash flooding across Louisiana on May 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media pictures showed flooding of several roadways across the city of New Orleans. In some cases, the water was 3 to 4 feet deep.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary stalled across the area. At the same time, the morning upper air sounding showed atmospheric moisture in the column to be well over 2 inches. High moisture combined with a slow moving focus created an environment conducive for heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of a few streets impassable and water threatening to enter homes. Measured 3.19 inches at a pumping station and 2.62 inches at another station. Most fell within an hour.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of summer thunderstorms along numerous outflow boundaries delivered damaging wind gusts across portions of southeastern Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two homes had water enter into structures. Measured 5.98 inches at a pumping station between 1130PM and 230PM.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of summer thunderstorms along numerous outflow boundaries delivered damaging wind gusts across portions of southeastern Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Destrehan pumping station measured 6.89 inches. Luling pumping station 3.91 inches. Rest of parish between 2 and 3 inches. Numerous streets flooded and several homes inundated.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy thunderstorms developing and training along outflow boundaries led to heavy rainfall which caused flash flooding mainly across the Southshore of southeastern Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several areas throughout St. Charles Parish experiencing street flooding. Measured readings between 2 and 3.5 inches with heavy rain ongoing. 2 homes were taking in flood water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of summertime strong to severe thunderstorms developed and caused damaging winds along with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video relayed via social media showing street flooding along Airline Drive near Causeway Blvd.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of summertime strong to severe thunderstorms developed and caused damaging winds along with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of water in homes on Faye Avenue in Metairie. Many streets flooded across the area including water overtopping canals. Street flooding in River Ridge.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of summertime strong to severe thunderstorms developed and caused damaging winds along with heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported feet of water in Waggaman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water near the intersection of Scenic Highway and Hollywood street made it impassable.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Delta first developed into a tropical depression in the Caribbean just south of Jamaica on the afternoon of October 4th. As it tracked across the western Caribbean, it rapidly intensified into a Category 4 hurricane. In fact, intensifying from tropical depression to Category strength in 40 hours is the fastest rate of intensification of any storm on record in the Atlantic Basin. Delta quickly weakened to a category 1 hurricane after making its first landfall on the Yucatan Peninsula. It gradually recurved north towards the Louisiana coastline, fluctuating in intensity between Category 2 and 3. Hurricane Delta ultimately arrived with an intensity of 105 mph, a strong category 2 storm. Local impacts include 50 to 70 MPH wind gusts across the area, storm surge of 2 to 3 feet above ground and widespread tree and structural damage. There were 6 injuries. In addition, outer bands of Delta produced a significant amount of rainfall on the north side of Baton Rouge Metro. Upwards of 5 to 10 inches of rain fell, causing street flooding in Baton Rouge and moderate river flooding in the region. Delta caused approximately $100 million worth of damage across southeast Louisiana. ||It made landfall around 5 pm east of Cameron LA or about 15 miles east of where Category 4 Hurricane Laura made landfall just a couple of months earlier this year. Delta became the 4th named storm to make landfall in Louisiana this hurricane season.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water made Greenwell Springs impassable at the intersections of Frenchtown and North Flannery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the daytime hours on March 23rd through March 24th, a deep west coast trough helped to deliver progressive yet strong southwesterly upper-level to mid-level moist flow across the northern Gulf coast. At the surface, a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms approached the area from the west, quickly advancing east causing widespread severe thunderstorms and areas of flash flooding. Another round of thunderstorms developed during the day on March 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southeast Search and Rescue reported water over 2 feet deep on Sofia Street in River Ridge.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through the region on the 8th of April. Ample moisture in place combined with strong lift along the frontal boundary set the stage for several severe storms producing damaging winds, some flash flooding and a brief tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kenner emergency management relayed a report from police that the intersection of Chateau and Vintage was impassable.

This drastically enhanced downdraft winds, producing long duration winds around tropical storm strength across the area with peak gusts as strong as a weak hurricane.

Ample overnight instability and weak steering flow resulted in damaging wind gusts, flash flooding and a few isolated weak tornadoes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported flood waters covering the road and encroaching on homes.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought a cold front to the region. Ahead of this cold front, moisture levels through the atmospheric column increased to above the 90th percentile for this time of year. Ample instability in place combine with the abundant moisture and lift from the frontal boundary set the stage for numerous thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon and evening hours on July 2nd, a stalled frontal boundary draped across northern Louisiana, central Mississippi and Alabama. This left moist, unstable air across southern Louisiana and much drier continental air to the north. This boundary, along with a upper-level weak disturbance helped ignite slow-moving scattered showers and thunderstorms that led to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening hours on July 27th, 2021, a disorganized cluster of showers and storms along a southward surging outflow boundary pushed heavy thunderstorms into portions of southern and southeastern Louisiana. Ample total atmospheric moisture content and instability led to intense rainfall rates ultimately causing areas of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding occurred in a few parts of the parish, though no homes were reported to take on water. One man drowned when he drove into a flooded underpass on I-10.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ninth named storm, fourth hurricane, and second major hurricane of the 2021 Atlantic hurricane season, Ida originated from a tropical wave in the Caribbean Sea on August 23rd. On August 26th, the wave developed into a tropical depression, which organized further and became Tropical Storm Ida later that day, near Grand Cayman. On a northwestward track, Ida intensified into a hurricane on August 27, just before moving over western Cuba. A day later, the hurricane underwent rapid intensification over the Gulf of Mexico as it passed over a warm core eddy and reached major hurricane strength. It was just under 72 hours from tropical depression formation to category 4 strength with 150 mph winds. Ida remained at its peak intensity of 150 mph winds and a minimum central pressure of 929 millibars as it made landfall near Port Fourchon midday on August 29th. It didn���t weaken to a tropical storm until it reached near the Louisiana/Mississippi border. ||Ida produced extensive to catastrophic damage across southeast Louisiana. On Grand Isle, around 40% of the structures were destroyed and the island was uninhabitable. Near 75% of the structures in Lafourche Parish were damaged or destroyed and likely similar numbers in Terrebonne Parish. 100+ mph wind gusts reached as far inland as Interstate 12. These winds damaged more than 30,000 poles, over 36,000 spans of wire and nearly 6,000 transformers. Of those more than 30,000 Louisiana poles, nearly 80% of those broken or damaged are from the most heavily impacted areas.|In total, the number of damaged or destroyed poles from Ida is more than hurricanes Katrina, Ike, Delta and Zeta combined. Due to major destruction to the power grid across the region, over 1 million customers were without power after landfall. Some of those power outages near the Louisiana coast lasted for around 4 weeks. |||The Louisiana Department of Health reported 3 direct fatalities and 28 indirect fatalities. Excessive heat and carbon monoxide poisoning were the most common causes of death. Indirect injuries associated with Ida: 351 heat-related, 183 carbon monoxide exposure, and 3 electrocutions. Storm surge ranged from a few feet to 10 to 15 feet. Hurricane Ida caused approximately $30 billion worth of damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell throughout the parish causing significant street flooding. At least a few homes in Harahan suffered minor water intrusion from the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Nicholas made landfall on the Texas coast south of Houston early Tuesday morning on September 14th. Over the next 24 hours, the weakening storm slowly tracked east-northeast along the coastline, reaching the LA/TX border Wednesday morning. During the 14th and 15th, outer bands of Nicholas expanded east across southeast Louisiana. The persistent rain from those banding features produced areas of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: East Baton Rouge Parish emergency manager reported closure of I-110 at the governors mansion curve due to high water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures soared into the low 80s, nearly breaking record highs for the day, just ahead of an approaching cold front. These warm and moist conditions created ample instability that, when combined with increasing wind shear from upper level system just north of the region, supported the development of severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall. This system spawned one tornado and scattered areas of wind damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local street water height gauge measured water up to 3 feet at the underpass of I-610 and Paris Avenue.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the local area began to slow down its eastward progression as it reached southeast Louisiana. Thunderstorms along the boundary started training over the same areas due to the slowing down of the cold front. This resulted in multiple areas of flash flooding across the region. Property damage was fairly minimal but dozens of streets across different cities became impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Acadian Thruway underpass was closed due to high water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved into north-central Texas on March 22nd. Thunderstorms began developing in the unstable warm sector ahead of a cold front moving east into east Texas and western LA. Very strong low and mid level winds ahead of the cold front brought in ample low level moisture with dewpoints reaching into the 70's near the coast. As temperatures at the surface warmed into the 80s and temps aloft dropped, the atmosphere steadily became unstable. Increasing shear combined with the very unstable atmosphere set the stage for 5 tornadoes to develop, one of them was violent, resulting in 2 fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local emergency manager reported several streets and a few underpasses to be closed due to high water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the evening hours of April 13th. As the front reached the New Orleans metro, it slowed slightly. This slowing along with a fairly wide swath of intense rainfall resulted in street flooding across the city of New Orleans.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received an MPING report of water covered roadways near Edward Herbert Boulevard.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving through the region on the 25th. A broken line of thunderstorms developed along the frontal boundary. A few storms developed ahead of the main line of storms for a short period before the main line caught up to them. This resulted in a longer period of rainfall for some areas of New Orleans metro which lead to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received an MPING report of water covered roadways near Hollywood Park Drive.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving through the region on the 25th. A broken line of thunderstorms developed along the frontal boundary. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported Dickory Road to be flooded.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that was moving south across Mississippi and Louisiana on 6/9 moved across portions of the the local coastal waters. Sufficient instability was still in place from the previous afternoon to support severe thunderstorms along the boundary. It then stalled across the coastal waters near the mouth of the Mississippi River on the morning of the 10th. Not long after stalling, it began to lift back north as a warm front. As it did so, storms began training across New Orleans metro, resulting in flash flooding. Moving into the afternoon hours, the warm front continued north. Daytime heating destabilized the atmosphere even more within the warm sector, south of the front. This provided an environment which supported severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts over both land and marine areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported widespread street flooding across the city of New Orleans with some roads closed due to high water.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was centered north and west of the region. That put the local area on the outer periphery of the ridge. Atmospheric moisture levels were very high, with a precipitable water value of 2.4 inches. That value is right at the average highest for this time of year. An easterly wave passing across the local area on the 5th provided the focus for convective development and was able to capitalize on the abundant moisture in place. One of the storms that developed was able to produce heavy rainfall resulting in flash flooding across New Orleans.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported numerous flooded streets in the Mid-City neighborhood.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a fairly typical summer-time regime in place with abundant moisture available and warm temperatures expected. Convergence along sea-breeze boundaries sparked off thunderstorm development. It was along these boundaries that a thunderstorm produced severe gusts in marine areas. That same storm grew westward from Lake Borne across the city of New Orleans. In doing so, movement was slow and thus flash flooding occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Acadian Thruway underpass was impassable due to high water. Report relayed via social media.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meso-scale convective system (MCS) developed out of afternoon thunderstorms initiated along a frontal boundary in northern Alabama and Georgia on the 21st. That MCS raced south across southern Alabama and Mississippi during the overnight hours, moving across the local area on the morning of the 22nd. There was also flash flooding in the Baton Rouge Metro due to the portion of the squall line associated with the MCS slowing down as it moved through there. In addition, a home and junior high gym caught fire after lightning strikes. No injuries reported with those fires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Greenwell Springs Road at Airline Highway was impassable due to high water. Report relayed via social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported street flooding near Southern University Campus.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge that was centered just north of the area began to slide east. As it did so, it both allowed for decreased subsidence aloft and Gulf moisture to move in. Daytime heating combined with precipitable water values rising to 2.2+ inches set the stage for a couple thunderstorms to produce flash flooding in Baton Rouge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported road flooding near Highway 61 and Scenic Highway.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge that was centered just north of the area began to slide east. As it did so, it both allowed for decreased subsidence aloft and Gulf moisture to move in. Daytime heating combined with precipitable water values rising to 2.2+ inches set the stage for a couple thunderstorms to produce flash flooding in Baton Rouge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media shared a video of street flooding at the intersection of Joseph Street and Tchoupitoulas.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was centered east of the area and another to the west. That put the local area in a region of weakness between them which is an area conducive for higher rain chances. The upper air soundings around that time showed sufficient instability and moisture in place to support numerous thunderstorm development. One storm developed around New Orleans, but didn���t move much despite the general flow of storms to be northwest at around 10 mph. Getting hung up on a sea-breeze boundary was the likely culprit of the storm not moving and producing flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported along Jefferson Highway in Baton Rouge.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was centered east of the area and another to the west. That put the local area in a region of weakness between them which is an area conducive for higher rain chances. The upper air soundings around that time showed sufficient instability and ample moisture in place to support numerous thunderstorm development. The local precipitable water value was well over 2 inches which provided the potential for heavy rainfall. One storm developed around Baton Rouge in the afternoon. For a short period, new development occurred in the same location while the storm itself moved northward. That non-movement and thus continued heavy rain resulted in flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at the intersection of North Roberston and Kentucky Street.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical upper-tropospheric trough (TUTT) low was moving westward across the northern Gulf of Mexico. This feature brought with it, abundant moisture throughout the column as well as a focus for storms to develop. Heavy rain and flash flooding occurred in St. Bernard Parish as the TUTT low moved into the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were stalled in flood waters on Burbank near the Walkons restaurant.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall in the preceding 7 days set the stage for the potential for flash flooding as many areas had rain soaked grounds. The morning upper air sounding at KLIX showed a precipitable water value of 2.2, which is near the climatological max for this time of year. As slow moving thunderstorms developed over the Baton Rouge metro, high rainfall rates quickly overcame local drainage capacity of wet grounds, resulting in flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a video of water up the sides of cars on Plantation Road.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ongoing rainy pattern that had been in place for a week or more continued on August 9th. The seabreeze boundary on the south side of Lake Ponchartrain became the focus for thunderstorms to develop around the New Orleans Metro. Due to the fact that these storms developed on the seabreeze, their movement was slow. This, combined with abundant moisture in place, resulted in flash flooding at a few locations in Jefferson Parish.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of street flooding in Woodmere.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was continuing rainy pattern that had been in place for a week or more. A wide swath of showers and thunderstorms developed in the coastal waters just south of Louisiana coast. As those storms moved northward within a very moisture rich atmosphere, they were both enhanced by the seabreeze boundary located across the City of New Orleans and slowed in progression. This resulted in flash flooding in portions of Jefferson and Orleans parishes.

One of those storms spread northward towards the Baton Rouge Metro and began to train over the area. A notoriously poor drainage underpass was overcome by the heavy rain and became impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding around 1.5 feet deep near Napoleon and Camp. Time estimated by radar.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary that stalled across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi on September 2nd, slowly lifted northward through the day on September 3rd. Abundant moisture in place south of the boundary provided enough lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop. The existence and slow movement of the front resulted in slow moving storms. This resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding over portions of Baton Rouge and New Orleans.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding from heavy rainfall was reported near the base of the Wisner overpass.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and surface low produced several supercell thunderstorms with embedded tornadoes across Southeast Louisiana and Southern Mississippi during the late afternoon and evening hours of Wednesday December 14, 2022. These supercell thunderstorms produced straight line wind damage and a several tornadoes in the area. Two EF-2 tornados impacted portions of the New Orleans metro area in St. Charles Parish, and in Jefferson, Orleans, and St. Bernard Parishes.||6 tornadoes have been confirmed from this event. These tornadoes have been determined to range in strength from EF0 to EF2 after NWS storm surveys were conducted. The St. Charles tornado resulted in 8 injuries and 1 fatality.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over numerous roadways and side streets in Denham Springs.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms approached the local area from the north and stalled along and just north of the I-12 corridor. The heaviest storms along the stalled line set up near Denham Springs. This led to flash flooding as the line sat there. The storms weakened and eventually dissipated an hour an a half later, and the flooding was allowed to go subside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over the road which looks like from a creek overflowing. Lane looks impassable.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface pressure trough, which later turned into a stationary front, situated over the Gulf of Mexico slowly crept northward until it reached the I-12 corridor near Baton Rouge. There, with abundant moisture in place, heavy showers developed along it as the front as it stalled right over Baton Rouge. Heavy downpours from stationary showers and storms produced flash flooding throughout the Baton Rouge metro and its surrounding suburbs on the morning of the 29th. By late-afternoon, the showers and storms finally weakened and dissipated, allowing the flooding to subside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported impassable streets across Bridge City.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low and frontal boundary stalled over northeast Texas on the afternoon of February 7th. An upper level low coming out of the desert southwest tracked eastward and drove a cold front towards the local area. At the same time, the surface low began to deepen and occlude while moving northeastward. Thunderstorms developed both ahead of the front in the warm sector and directly along the cold front as it passed through the local area. One storm produced an EF-2 tornado, another severe thunderstorm winds, and one produced flash flooding. No injuries with any storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos shared on social media by broadcast media showed water approximately 2 feet deep in the Sweetbriar trailer park on Samuels Road in Zachary. At least one car was flooded.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached the local area from the west, the environment became primed for thunderstorms to develop with warmth and instability increasing. Storms that developed ahead of the front had little mid level flow to give them much movement. Thus, one storm over the Baton Rouge metro produced heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water reported on Scenic Highway on the north side of Baton Rouge.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was in place for a couple days across the region. This feature was the focal point for numerous showers and thunderstorms. Weak mid level flow aided in slow movement of storms that did develop. One of those stronger slow moving storms produced flash flooding around Baton Rouge and hail in Galliano.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car is stalled on Metairie road westbound with water over the westbound lanes near Metairie Lawn cemetery in Metairie.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more typical summertime pattern was in place on May 9th as scattered showers and thunderstorms started to develop during the late morning. With the help of colliding sea-breeze and lake-breeze boundaries over New Orleans, a thunderstorm developed over the city in the late morning. The storm rooted itself to the sea-breeze boundary and became nearly stationary over the city. The storm remained over the city for nearly 4 hours, causing considerable flash flooding, especially in the Gentilly area of New Orleans before dissipating in the late afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooded street report at Ursulines Avenue and N Claiborne Avenue.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fueled by an abundance of moisture, a thunderstorm complex developed during peak heating over the Pearl River basin. Aided by a very weak steering flow, the complex moved very slowly over portions of urbanized areas such as Slidell and eventually metro New Orleans, where flash flooding occurred during the afternoon and evening. The complex finally weakened during the evening as it became cold-pool dominant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low formed across the ArkLaTex region in the early morning hours on December 1st. In the warm sector of the low, thunderstorms formed along a convergent axis almost parallel to the upper-level flow, which caused training to occur over an urban area (Baton Rouge). That same batch of convection eventually cold-pooled out and raced east when a bowing segment produced a very brief tornado in Pearl River county, aided by a surging rear-inflow jet. After that convection exited the area, another batch formed during the late evening hours, with one cell becoming dominant and producing golf ball sized hail before the whole complex weakened.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of streets flooded across metro New Orleans, with most reports in the Mid City and Uptown areas. A few reports of underpasses closed at Carrolton and Canal.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of December 2nd, thunderstorms ignited along a convergent axis parallel to the upper-level flow. This set up over urbanized New Orleans, and caused flash flooding that morning. As that complex moved eastward, a strong cell moved over Lake Pontchartrain and produced a strong wind gust over the southern part of the lake. That cell was the last of the storms that day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple streets across Gretna were impassable due to high water from heavy rainfall.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low developed in the northwestern Gulf of Mexico. As this low and developing cold front appendant to it tracked east along the northern Gulf Coast, a wide swath of showers and thunderstorms moved across southern Louisiana. Increasing buoyancy from pressure falling as the surface low moved in closer convective robustness of thunderstorms. The duration of heavy rain associated with this system resulted in flash flooding across portions of metro New Orleans.

The main focus of heavier rain was on the south shore of Lake Pontchartrain. Slow storm movement and moderate to heavy rain rates resulted in multiple areas of flash flooding across New Orleans Metro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance, surface low, and cold front produced a line of severe thunderstorms that pushed across Southeast Louisiana and Southern Mississippi in the morning and early afternoon hours of Wednesday, April 10, 2024. The line of severe thunderstorms produced strong straight line winds,  extensive flash flooding in the New Orleans metro area and several tornadoes. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 tornado that impacted the city of Slidell in St. Tammany Parish, LA on the morning of the 10th. 7 tornadoes have been confirmed from this event. 4 of those 7 touched down and tracked across just the city of Slidell. At one point, as many as 3 of those 7 were on the ground at the same time. These tornadoes have been determined to range in strength from EFU to EF2 after NWS storm surveys and high resolution satellite analysis were conducted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continual progressive flow aloft coupled with unstable air ignited thunderstorms across southeastern Texas during the afternoon hours. These storms formed into a linear complex and traversed along a northward advancing warm front draped across the gulf coast. Aided by ample instability and favorable wind shear, this cluster organized into a damaging wind with embedded tornado risk across southeastern Louisiana. Multiple rounds of storms also aided in flash flooding across urbanized areas. The complex continued to dive southward, favoring the highest instability and continued in to the coastal waters off of Mississippi. This complex continued to produce strong wind gusts until it exited the coastal waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of several inches of standing water near the intersection of St. Joseph St and Convention Center Blvd via social media.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea-breeze and lake-breeze boundaries, along with favorable instability, triggered thunderstorm development during the late morning and afternoon timeframe. These storms produced strong winds across the waters during that timeframe. These storms were also slow-moving, so urban areas saw some flash flooding during the morning. With no wind shear, these storms dissipated a couple hours later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a video of sidewalk and street flooding in the French Quarter.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection initiation on June 1st was fairly typical summertime with storms developing along sea breeze boundaries in the late morning hours. These storms began to grow upscale as daytime heating persisted combined with ample instability and moisture in place. Storms became more widespread and intense as a more organized complex moved into the local forecast area from southwest Louisiana. A handful of storms intensified enough to produce severe hail and damaging winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of water covered roads across Metairie Heights Ave.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime convective pattern was in place with high moisture and weak steering currents. Much of the convection was determined by mesoscale boundaries, which set up over New Orleans. This caused a storm to sit over that populated area, flooding some streets before dissipating shortly after.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms formed during the afternoon across southeast Louisiana in response to high moisture and instability. This cluster of storms were able to take advantage of mid-level dry air to produce severe winds across the Florida parishes and marine areas before pushing into the southshore and producing flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 4-6 inches of water over the roadway in Harahan, New Orleans, Louisiana.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms initiated across south-central Louisiana in response to high instability. This cluster of thunderstorms moved eastward while interacting with mid-level dry air. That dry air helped produced severe downdraft winds as the cluster moved through southeast Louisiana, southern Mississippi, and the adjacent marine areas. This complex also produced heavy rain in New Orleans which led to some flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed impassable roads across Lakeview, just west of City Park.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level centered over Texas began driving moisture into the local area a couple days prior to this event. By the 30th, the atmospheric moisture content had reached values near the record for this time of year. Storms that developed in this environment were able to tap into this moisture to produce quite heavy rainfall and when combined with slow storm movement, produced flash flooding over New Orleans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled boundary along the LA coast in association with a stationary low in the northeast Gulf created multiple rounds of heavy rain. This caused some isolated flash flooding along the I-12 corridor in Baton Rouge and St. Tammany Parish.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads barricaded due to deep water in the Destrehan area.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After multiple days of off and on rain from the stalled boundary along the LA coast, more rain in association with that boundary fell across southeast Louisiana. This time, the heaviest rain fell closer to the coast in portions of Terrebonne and St. Charles parish where instances of flash flooding occured.

This time, the heaviest rain fell closer to the coast in portions of Terrebonne and St. Charles parish where instances of flash flooding occured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of street flooding on Prospect Ave. in Norco shared via social media.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After multiple days of off and on rain from the stalled boundary along the LA coast, more rain in association with that boundary fell across southeast Louisiana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greatest impact in the parish was flooding caused by heavy rain.  As the north and northeastern eyewall stretched into an east-west oriented band of heavy rainfall, training storms resulted in widespread rainfall totals of 6-9��� across the parish with locally higher amounts. Most of the rain fell over the course of just a couple hours, overwhelming the city���s drainage capacity and causing canals to overflow. Numerous roads were impassable, and some underpasses flooded with up to 8 ft of water. One high water rescue was necessary when a man drove his vehicle around a barricade and into an underpass. He was transported to an area hospital following the rescue.
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Francine formed as a tropical storm in the Gulf of Mexico on September 9, 2024, and intensified into a Category 2 hurricane before making landfall near Houma, Louisiana, on the morning of September 11. With sustained winds of 100 mph and gusts peaking at 105 mph, Francine brought widespread damage to southeastern Louisiana and southern Mississippi. A storm surge of 6-9 feet inundated low-lying coastal areas, while torrential rainfall of 10-15 inches caused significant flash flooding, including in the New Orleans metro area. Over 400,000 residents experienced power outages, and extensive wind damage was reported, with numerous trees and powerlines downed. There were a total of 6 direct injuries, 0 indirect injuries and 0 fatalities. The Louisiana Department of Health reported 18 ED visits for carbon dioxide exposure across various parishes in southeast Louisiana. In terms of dollar damage, the best estimate around $100 million but that could be very conservative as some online reports suggest much more than that.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greater impact was from Francine���s flooding rain, though. As the north and northeastern eyewall stretched into an east-west oriented band of heavy rainfall, training storms resulted in widespread rainfall totals of 7-9��� across the parish with locally higher amounts, much of which fell over the course of just a couple hours. The heavy rain resulted in significant flash flooding across the parish, with numerous areas becoming inaccessible, and approximately over 350 homes and businesses being flooded throughout the parish, with the worst flooding reported in Norco.
